To access the openlab servers, you must either connect to campus using the UCI VPN (which may add noticeable latency) or use SSH key-based authentication.
Set Up SSH Keys
Note
:  These commands are intended to be run on Linux or Apple hosts but they were tested on the
Openlab Linux Cluster
.
You must be connected to the campus VPN in order to log in to the Openlab Linux Cluster without an SSH key.
Other Resources
Outside resources may have more relevant or contextually correct information than the ICS provided directions.
https://www.cyberciti.biz/faq/how-to-set-up-ssh-keys-on-linux-unix/
Creating and Using SSH in Windows
How to Use ssh-keygen to Generate a New SSH Key?
SSH Jumphost:
Using a Jumphost
X11 Display forwarding:
https://kb.iu.edu/d/bdnt
Git
Gitlab
github
Set Up keys on the Openlab Linux Cluster
Generate keys with ssh-keygen
Use the
ssh-keygen
command to generate a set of SSH keys suitable for logging in in via ssh. This command will generate a private and public key combinations.
The default keyfile names are id_rsa and id_rsa.pub.  If this is your first SSH key then you may use the default names.  If you are creating additional keys, you will want to change the name of the file when prompted.
% ssh-keygen
Generating public/private rsa key pair.
Enter file in which to save the key (/home/icsuser/.ssh/id_rsa):
Enter passphrase (empty for no passphrase):
Enter same passphrase again:
Your identification has been saved in /home/icsuser/.ssh/id_rsa.
Your public key has been saved in /home/icsuser/.ssh/id_rsa.pub.
The key fingerprint is:
28:c8:58:6a:08:59:a0:fd:31:9d:8d:25:59:93:18:28 ics_user@openlab
Choose a strong passphrase.  Do not, under any circumstances, leave an empty passphrase.  Use ssh-agent in order to reduce the need to type in your passphrase.
Note the location of the key pair.  The public portion, the .pub file, must be copied to the server.  Your client will use the private portion of the key pair when connecting.
Add the public key to your Authorized Keys File
Now that you have our public/private key pair, add the public key pair into your ~/.ssh/authorized_keys file:
cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub>> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
or
echo "key" >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
Logging in with a Key
If the key is named id_rsa or id_dsa, then the key will be used automatically when running ssh.  If this key is named differently then you will need to specify which key to use when calling ssh:
ssh -i ~/.ssh/altkeyname_rsa icsuser@openlab.ics.uci.edu
SSH Agent
You can use the ssh-agent command to automate access to ICS computing, ICS Gitlab, Group Accounts, and more.
The best resources are probably located outside of the ICS wiki but we'll try to provide ICS specific information.
Here are a starting point for general usage:
https://www.ssh.com/academy/ssh/agent
Starting the SSH Agent
Start the ssh-agent on your computure by running your preferred shell, in this case bash:
ICS% ssh-agent bash
Adding Keys
You can add the your default private key, typically ~/.ssh/id_rsa, by simply running the ssh-add command at the prompt:
ICS% ssh-add
You will be prompted for the key's passphrase.
If you have additional keys that you use, for exmaple for github.com, you can add them to your ssh key chain as well:
ICS% ssh-add id_rsa.github
Using the keys
Now, when invoking ssh related commands (e.g. ssh, scp, sftp, git), the ssh aware program will try the keys in your ssh key chain before prompting you for a password.
SSH Groupleader Accounts
SSH keys can be used to access any account, including
Groupleader
(a.k.a. gsu) accounts.  The principal is the same, add your public half of your key pair to the ssh accounts authorized_keys file.   Follow similar steps to those above:
Create the private/public key pair
Add the public portion of the key pair to the groupleader account authorized_key file.
ssh using the new key.
Creating Groupleader Key pair
This is the same process as creating your own key pair.   Execute the `ssh-keygen` as the user on the host you will be ssh from.  The process is the same for any Linux host, both ICS and your own personal system.
In the following example, replace “your_user” with your own local username and icsgroupleader with the name of the groupleader account you wish to ssh into.
% ssh-keygen
Generating public/private rsa key pair.
Enter file in which to save the key (/home/your_user/.ssh/id_rsa): /home/your_user/.ssh/icsgroupleader_rsa
Enter passphrase (empty for no passphrase):
Enter same passphrase again:
Your identification has been saved in /home/your_user/.ssh/icsgroupleader_rsa.
Your public key has been saved in /home/your_user/.ssh/icsgroupleader_rsa.
The key fingerprint is:
28:c8:58:6a:08:59:a0:fd:31:9d:8d:25:59:93:18:28 ics_user@openlab
The
private
~/.ssh/icsgroupleader_rsa and the
public
~/.ssh/icsgroupleader_rsa.pub are created.
Keep the
private
portion of the key secure.   It should be set mode 600 (`chmod 600 ~/.ssh/ics_groupleader_rsa).
Add the public portion of the key pair
Add the public key into the gsu or groupleader account's authorized_keys file.   Often times it is easiest to open a shell on an ICS host as yourself, use
gsu
become the groupleader account and then copy/paste the public portion of the key into the groupleader's ~/.ssh/authorized_keys file.
The public portion is typically one line long and begins with
ssh-rsa
SSH as the Groupleader user
At this point you should be able use your new key to authenticate as the icsgroupleader account.  The command will typically look like this:
your_user@your_machine% ssh -i ~/.ssh/icsgroupleader_rsa icsgroupleader@openlab.ics.uci.edu
You can also use this key in many applications that use ssh to connect to ICS servers including vscode, cyberduck, and moboxterm.
SSH Key Special Restrictions
Adding this to the front of the SSH key will restrict the user of the key to only sftp connections:
command="internal-sftp"
Adding this to the front of the SSH key will only allow specific hosts to connect using the key:
from="<ip addresses comma delimited>"
Generating a Putty Private Key on Windows
Follow these
directions
to convert your key to a format usable by Putty (if you choose another SFTP client, google the relevant directions for that particular client):
or these
directions
(note the need to convert keys generated on the ICS servers)
Using your Keys on Putty
Check:
Putty
The known_hosts file
Linux
Location:  ~/.ssh/known_hosts
The ssh known_hosts file is a file that stores the public key of all of the servers that you have connected using ssh. This file is used to verify the identity of servers in the future. Ssh will automatically add keys to this file, but they can be added manually as well.
Sometimes, ucommonly but when hosts are reinstalled for example, a servers' public key can change.  When this happens you may recieve a warning or an error that a key in your known_hosts file doesn't match the on the server.   In order to reconcile this conflict, you can use the `ssh-keygen -R` command.   For example `ssh-keygen -R openlab.ics.uci.edu` will remove the openlab.ics.uci.edu key  from your known_hosts file.  The new one will be installed the next time you ssh into openlab.
Putty
https://www.simplified.guide/putty/edit-delete-ssh-hosts-keys
MobaXTerm
Troubleshooting
Can't Login with My Key
Check permissions.  The home directory should be no better than 751.  The .ssh directory should be 700 and the authorized key file should be 600.
WARNING: REMOTE HOST IDENTIFICATION HAS CHANGED!
You may receive this error when an important event has occurred in the lifecycle of the ICS Computing host, such as host reinstallation or
OS
upgrade.  Typically this is coupled with a a message that you have requested strict checking.
Check to make sure that your connection to the network is secure and that the your
DNS
queries are not being hijacked.  If everything looks good, then clear the old key from your known_hosts file.
Linux and Macintosh
The ssh-keygen has a built-in command for accomplishing this:
ssh-keygen -R hostname
If this does not work, open up your ~/.ssh/known_hosts file and remove all of the lines that refer to the offending host and host-ip address.  Typically, the error message you receive will have pointers so read it carefully.  Twice.
Additional Systems
Clearing the known_hosts SSH File
SSH stops working on MacOS Ventura
Some uses may experience problems connecting via ssh to ICS resources when running MacOS:Ventura.  This is due to the fact that RSA keys have been disabled by default.  You can follow the instructions at the following site to re-enable them:
https://www.droidwin.com/ssh-not-working-in-macos-ventura-fix/

What gets backed up?

Backups at ICS
Snapshots
A Snapshot copy is a read-only image of a disk or volume that captures the state of the file system at a point in time.  We can think of these snapshots as “back up” copies.  Most ICS Network File Servers (NFS) and Samba servers maintain regular snapshots for /home and /extra directories.  The snapshot schedule and availability differ from device to device and volume to volume, but most commonly 30 daily snapshots are kept.
Tardigrade Purestorage Flashblade
Most home and extra directories are store on
Tardigrade
.  Snapshots are available on most filesystems.   Typically, snapshots are taken daily and retained  for 28 days.  Occasionally, when significant changes are made to the filesystem, it is necessary to delete snapshots early.
Snapshots are
read-only
and they
do not
contribute to quota'd disk usage.
How to use Snapshots to Recover Files
For Linux systems, cd into the hidden directory:
~/.snapshot
On Windows 10, use the following on
This PC
's address bar:
H:\.snapshot where H is the drive letter for
samba
drives, like your home directory
S:\.snapshot where S is the drive letter for
samba
shared drives
If the above does not work for you, try mapping the snapshot folder directly:
Right click on
This PC
(Windows 10)
Select
Map Network Drive
or go to the
Computer
tab and select
Map Network Drive
For
Drive
, choose an available drive letter
For
Folder
, use:
\\samba.ics.uci.edu\youICSusername\.snapshot
From MacOs, assuming you have your home directory mounted as follows:
smb://samba.ics.uci.edu/youICSusername
You will repeat the mapping steps (
Finder
,
Go
,
Connect to Server
) with:
smb://samba.ics.uci.edu/youICSusername/.snapshot
Network Appliance
Most home directories and some extra directories are stored on
Cyberton
.  Snapshots are available on most volumes. Typical snapshot policy is for 28 daily snapshots and 2 hourly snapshots.  Some volumes that see frequent or large changes in content will have fewer snapshots available.  Snapshots are read-only and do not contribute to your overall disk usage. These are provide for you to self-restore files you have accidentally overwritten or deleted.
How to Recover Files
For Linux systems, use:
the hidden directory ~/.snapshot
On Windows 10, use the following on
This PC
:
H:\~snapshot where H is the drive letter for
cybertron
drives, like your home directory
S:\.snapshot where S is the drive letter for
samba
shared drives
The .snapshot directory is hidden and will not be visible in the file explorer so you will press the Windows key and type in the drive letter and path (e.g. H:\.snapshot).
Alternatively, the full path may be entered into the file explorer's navigation bar:
where you would press
Enter
, look at the right pane, and navigate the folder structure until you find files you want to retrieve.  Please note that these backup folders are organized by date and time.  Once you find those files, copy them to another location.
ZFS NFS Appliances
Most ZFS NFS appliances keep 14 daily snapshots and 2 weekly snapshots.
These are user accessible via the /net automount path:
/net/<hostname>/<volume>/.zfs
You can determine the hostname and volume name using the ldapsearch command:
ldapsearch -x cn=hans1 nismapentry    
dn: cn=hans1,nismapname=auto_extra.byname,dc=ics,dc=uci,dc=edu
nismapentry: b4.ics.uci.edu:/b4_ics_tank/hans1
In this example, snapshots would be available at:
/net/b4.ics.uci.edu/v4_ics_tank/.zfs
Long Term Backup
In addition to nightly snapshots, most NFS storage space also gets backed up to tape. The software that we utilize is Spectrum Protect and backups are generally performed monthly.
Retention Period
Any data that gets backed up to tape will be kept for a maximum of two years.
What gets backed up?
Everything in your /home or /extra gets backed up. Some research space may have a different policy. Please check with your group or contact Helpdesk for details.
Not Backed Up Items
.snapshots folder
scratch folder
The folder can be located at any level. Any data you place in there will not be backed up. This is great if you are writing large amount of temporary data as it greatly reduce the burden on resources.
Files with extension *.vdi

The School of ICS provides an ICS home directory for

Tardigrade:  The Updated Storage Server
Beginning Fall 2021, all home directories are being stored on a new
Purestorage Flashblade
named
tardigrade.ics.uci.edu
, occasionally abbreviated as tg.ics.uci.edu.
Highlights:
Backed by all flash storage so home directory access is fast
Redundant 40Gb/s uplink to the ICS Data Center Backbone
Inline compression (data reduction factor of ~1.6)
28 days of self service snapshots
+ long term disaster recovery backups
Storage System
Typically, there are two types of storage systems at ICS.
Network File System
Network file system, NFS, is available on every managed ICS system. On Linux, NFS is managed by the automounter (autofs) service. Anything under /home, /extra/, or /auto is NFS and autofs managed. On Windows or Mac systems, the space is available via CIFS or SMB and mapped as a network drive. This is also included in /home/ and /extra/.
NFS Pros:
Available everywhere
Backups
Hourly snapshots
Access control
Quotas
NFS cons:
Only as fast as the network
Expensive:  RAID + Snapshots + Fast Network are not cheap
Local File System
Local file systems are backed by local physical media and are only available on filesystems they are physically attached to. Most systems have local storage exposed for all users in /scratch or /tmp. Some faculty and researchers purchase extra and that's made available via /srv/disk# or srv/nvme#.
LocalFS Pros:
Cheap
Fast
Abundant
LocaFS Cons:
Scratch only, no backup
Localhost only.
Available over network via slower protocols, fuse or sshfs

How much space am I using?
Are you getting an over quota message but when running
df -h
it shows that there TBs of available space?
# df . -h
Filesystem         Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
tardigrade:/ugrad   99T   13T   86T  13% /net/tardigrade/ugrad
The command is returning the size of physical filesystem. Your quota is what is listed in the table above. If you would like to know how much space you're using, please run
du -sh /home/your_username
. This command could take a few minutes depending on how much data is in your home directory.
Backup and Recovery
Snapshots
Self service
Snapshots
are available to all users for file recovery for the previous four weeks (28 days).
Long Term Backups/Disaster Recovery
Backups to tape are made once per month.   Our goals is to keep a quarterly copy of the each home directory on tape for two or more years.   Contact
ICS Computing Support Helpdesk
if you feel  you require a file recovery that is no available via snapshots.
Exceptions
Files or directories called “scratch” and some video and audio files will be excluded from backups.

Groupleader Accounts (Linux)
Groupleader accounts allow teams and research groups to share storage space, file ownership, processes and related administrative duties on
ICS managed Linux hosts
.
Group accounts:
Allow shared ownership of service processes (e.g. apache, MySQL, etc)
Allow shared ownership of files
Allow you to access their home directory (/home/groupAccountName) from any ICS managed host or any ICS unmanaged host via
sshfs/samba
Grant you access to ICS
network attached storage
such as home and extra directories (as opposed to local disk, which are available only from the host they are attached to)
Are created on space that is
backed up
Group accounts do
not
:
Have coincidental Gmail accounts
Have passwords (use
SSH keys
instead)
If you would like to be part of an existing group account or create a new group account, please send a request to helpdesk@ics.uci.edu.  For existing group accounts, the owner of the account will need to approve your request first.
Note
:  sudo is the preferred method of access for groupleader accounts on all systems.  The gsu command is no longer available on Ubuntu 20.04, CentOS8, and newer systems.  Read below for instructions on using the sudo command.
Group vs Groupleader Account
Do you need a groupleader account or just a group?
The key distinction between groupleader account and a group is that the groupleader account is going to be able to own processes on ICS-managed Linux hosts.  A Linux group will still allow you to share files between group members.
If your main goal is to make files available between team members, ask ICS Helpdesk to create a group and assign it as the primary group id for your team members.
If your team is interested in running processes as a common user, ask for a groupleader account.
Feel free to discuss your computing needs with helpdesk@ics.uci.edu.
Accessing Group Accounts using sudo - All OS types
Log in to one of the ICS hosts with your ICS credentials:
ssh openlab.ics.uci.edu
After you log in, type:
sudo -u <groupAccountName> -s
Optional, add the -E variable to preserve your environment variables:
sudo -E -s -u <groupAccountName>
You will then be prompted for a password.  Type your personal ICS password.  You have now become the groupAccountName user.  However, you may still be in your own home directory. 
To check if you are in the right place:
pwd
If you see /home/yourUsername, move to the group account's home directory:
cd /home/groupAccountName
Logging in as a Group Account
If you wish to ssh to the group account directly, please look at
creating SSH Keys and the instructions on how to use it
.
Usage Examples
Start Shell as Group User
The following command starts a shell as a user while preserving the environment variables.
sudo -u <group uid> -sE
For example, if I wanted to become the
ucibdl
user (substitute your own group uid in the following example):
sudo -u ucibdl -sE
Create Directory
sudo -u ucibdl mkdir -p /extra/ucibdl0/shared/data
Set a Group Sticky Bit
The group sticky bit is a special Linux permission that allows the directory to be group writable
but
prevents a user that is not the file creator from deleting.  For group
ucibdl
:
sudo -u ucibdl chmod 2770 /extra/ucibdl0/shared/data
Change Group Ownership
You can change the ownership of directory
data
to group
ucibdl
.
sudo -u ucibdl chgrp ucibdl /extra/ucibdl0/shared/data
Perform the operation recursively on the entire shared directory:
sudo -u ucibdl chgrp -R ucibdl /extra/ucibdl0
Check your Primary Group
All members of a group should have their primary group set to their research group (
ucibdl
for example) to efficient sharing across group members.  The
id
command will list your user id, primary group id (ugrad in this case), and other groups.
id panteater                                                                                                                                                    
uid=52515(panteater) gid=1001(ugrad) groups=1001(ugrad),60012(cs122b)
Please reach out to helpdesk@ics.uci.edu for help adding users to a group and/or setting a user's primary group.
Use Umask to Set Default Permissions
Instead of fixing permissions later, use umask to set default permissions for newly-created files and directories. This command works by stripping away permissions as files/directories are created. Let's check the current umask setting, which is set to no permissions for Group and Other:
$ umask
0077
If you wanted to grant 'all' permissions for the Owner and Group (most common setting for research teams), you would do:
$ umask 007
which will allow for read and write for files
and
read, write, and execute for directories.
If you want to change the default umask value instead of setting it every time you log in, add the umask command at the bottom of your .bash_profile file.
Accessing Group Accounts using gsu - CentOS 7
Log in to one of the ICS hosts with your ICS credentials:
ssh openlab.ics.uci.edu
After you log in, type:
gsu <groupAccountName>
If you get a “command not found” error, type:
module load gsu
For more information on this module, check
gsu module
.
If you get an error about the modules command, you may have deleted/broken your
DOT files
and need to restore them (breaking, for example, by commenting out the necessary line `source /opt/Modules/init/bash` in .bashrc if you are using bash).
If you get an error that says “you won't be doing that today,” please check that your group account name is entered correctly and try again.
If you still get an error, please send an email message to helpdesk@ics.uci.edu.  Screenshots are appreciated to help us troubleshoot.
You will then be prompted for a password.  Type your ICS password.  You have now become the groupAccountName user.  However, you may still be in your own home directory. 
To check if you are in the right place:
pwd
If you see /home/yourUsername, move to the group account's home directory:
cd /home/groupAccountName
Maintaining Linux Groups
If you would like to be part of an existing group or create a new group, please send a request to helpdesk@ics.uci.edu.  For existing groups, the owner of the group will need to approve your request first.   Groups:
allow you to share resources from any ICS host
are often used to set permissions for shared directories (learn more about permissions
here
)
come with no inherent storage space
As research members leave ICS, it is good practice to check with helpdesk@ics.uci.edu to ensure that group membership is updated accordingly.
Sharing Data with your Group
Once your group has been created, members have been added, and their primary group has been set (or a sticky bit is in place), it is time to find a location to share data.  This location could be your personal home directories (which are backed up but have a quota), group account home directory (also backed up), /extra directories (also backed up), local storage space like /scratch, Google Drive, and/or OneDrive.  Check this page for more information on
storage
.  Please speak to helpdesk@ics.uci.edu if you need a large amount of space.
Troubleshooting
Common Errors
If you're getting one of the below messages, you're probably on an Ubuntu host. Please make sure that you're using the
sudo -u <username> -s
command instead of gsu.
gsu: command not found
odin:~$ gsu
gsu: error while loading shared libraries: libldap-2.4.so.2: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory

Mounting Remote Directories(Samba/SSHFS/NFSv4)
Samba
SMBCLIENT
The smbclient can be used to mount a remote directory.  We provide the command to access your
ICS home directory
below.
smbclient //samba.ics.uci.edu/<username> --user=ics.uci.edu/<username>
Example: Use SMBClient to Mount Your Own Home Directory
Peter the Anteater is a fictional user, use your own username in place.
% smbclient //samba.ics.uci.edu/ptanteater2 --user=ics.uci.edu/panteater2

Password for [ICS.UCI.EDU\ptanteater2]:
SMBFS
Users that have sudo or root access on their Linux host, may use the command `mount -t cifs` or `mount.cifs` to mount a SAMBA directory to appear as part of the filesystem.
The username panteater is a place filler, replace it with your own username
Some linux systems will not resolve a hostname. This is why we convert it to an ip address using dig +short
sudo mount -t cifs //$(dig +short samba.ics.uci.edu)/panteater  \
    /tmp/samba.ics.uci.edu/panteater  \
    -o user=panteater,domain=ics.uci.edu
You will be prompted for a password. Enter your ICS account password.
if your Ubuntu host is complaining about mounting “read-only” or not prompting for a password, install the
cifs-utils
package
Other, non-ICS SAMBA drives can be similarly mounted:
DOMAIN=example.domain SAMBASERVER=samba.example.com SHARE=example && \
  ( [ -d /tmp/$USER/$SAMBASERVER ] || mkdir -p /tmp/$USER/$SAMBASERVER ) \
  && sudo mount -t cifs //$(dig +short $SAMBASERVER)/$SHARE /tmp/$USER/$SAMBASERVER \
  -o username=$USER,domain=$DOMAIN
Special Samba Mount Points
If a user wants access to someone else's home/extra directory at the root or a subdirectory, a special samba mount point needs to be added. For example, if Pat (username: pat) needs to access /extra/anteater0/subdir, a request needs to be made by Anteater to allow Pat access.
At that point, edit the smb.conf.supplemental file with the following format but with the correct path, force user, and valid users:
[smb-mount-name] comment = Special share for the <name> account path = <directory path to special mount point> force user = <name> valid users = <list of users who can mount> browseable = no public = no writable = yes printable = no create mask = 0644 directory mask = 0755
Once the “smb-mount-name” has been added, you can .
If it is a group account's extra directory and access is granted, then just add the user to the group account.
SSHFS
In computing, SSHFS (SSH Filesystem) is a filesystem client used to mount and interact with directories and files located on a remote server or workstation over a normal SSH connection. This is useful for users on Unix-like operating systems who need access to home or group directories available through our ICS servers.
Install SSHFS/FUSE
This step depends on your distribution. You basically install “sshfs” which installs FUSE as a dependency. The user probably needs to be added to the local “fuse” UNIX group. Once the software is installed, notice the lack of the “sudo” command. The mounting is all done in user space as the user.
Ubuntu:
http://help.ubuntu.com/community/SSHFS
Fedora:
http://fedorasolved.org/server-solutions/sshfs
Gentoo:
http://gentoo-wiki.com/HOWTO_FTP_Mount#FUSE
Automating the Connection:
http://www.linuxjournal.com/article/8904
FUSE Group Membership
Any FUSE users will need to be members of the fuse group. The fusermount program is setgid root. Only members of the fuse group can run sshfs.
The fuse membership is assigned by puppet in the local /etc/group file by including the “fuse” statement in the user class in the node's .pp file (/etc/puppet/manifests/nodes/ailab.pp):
class { user: rootpw => "RSH", netgroups => ['+@common_u::::::', '+@ailab_u:x:::::', '+::::::/sbin/nologin'], fuse => "fuse:*:493:vipada,hans,ihler,ailab" }
Mount a Remote Directory
In the following examples, please replace
icsuser
with your username
SSHFS Options
idmap=user: Makes sure the files will be owned by the local user
allow_other: Allow your non-root user access to the specific resource you're mounting
default_permissions: Allow kernel to check permissions, i.e., use the actual permissions on the remote filesystem. This allows prohibiting access to everybody otherwise granted by allow_other
Personal Workstation
The following command should be run on the remote system where you want to mount your home directory:
sshfs -o allow_other,idmap=user icsuser@openlab.ics.uci.edu:/home/icsuser /mnt/icsuser
ICS Managed Linux Host
If you are on an ICS system, please use something similar to the following instead:
sshfs -o default_permissions,idmap=user icsuser@openlab.ics.uci.edu:/home/icsuser /scratch/icsuser
Example
The following example is run on any ICS managed system and mounts a directory from a local disk on a remote machine, medusa.ics.uci.edu, as the user hankwu:
mkdir /scratch/hankwu;
 sshfs -o default_permissions,idmap=user hankwu@medusa.ics.uci.edu:/srv/disk00/hans /scratch/hankwu
Some of these settings can also be applied via the /etc/fuse.conf file.
Unmount Your Filesystem
Typically, a simple umount will work fine:
umount /scratch/hankwu
If that fails, the following usually works:
fusermount -u /scratch/mountpoint
Example
fusermount -u /scratch/hankwu
ICS Group Leader Accounts
If the space you need to access  is owned by a
group leader account
, first set up
SSH keys
for the group leader account.
Once the key is set up in the group account, then use the following command:
sshfs -o "IdentityFile=$HOME/.ssh/yourkeyfile" <user>@<server>:<file_path> $HOME/mount-local
NFSv4
ICS Home Directory SSHFS and SMBFS
Troubleshooting
Large File Size Support
An issue you can have:
localuser@local-desktop:/mnt/mountpoint$ dd if=/dev/zero of=onegig bs=1024 count=2100000 File size limit exceeded (core dumped)
There's no large file support because SMB is very similar to the FAT file system.
DD Write dd if=/dev/zero of=onegig bs=1024 count=1000000 (1.0 GB) copied, 722.577 seconds, 1.4 MB/s DD Read dd if=onegig of=/dev/null bs=1024 (1.0 GB) copied, 252.435 seconds, 4.1 MB/s
IOZone ./iozone -Rac -i 0 -i 1 -b data2.xls Writes average is: 3416.452381 KB/s Re-writes average is: 3791.206349 KB/s Reads average is: 3942.507937 KB/s Re-reads average is: 868029.6429 KB/s
Transport Endpoint Not Connected
Problem:
error: failed to lstat /lv_tmp/tmp/mh: Transport endpoint is not connected ?--------- ? ? ? ? ? mh
Solution:
fusermount -u /tmp/mh
Hanging
If you are mounting to an unsupported system and the mount hangs, check that you are not mounting over an open file. In the past we had one user that had a HISTFILE set to a file in the directory that was being mounted over.
Performance
These tests were probably last performed in 2011, so it is time to revisit them.
“Unless you are doing a lot of re-reads, SSHFS is at least twice as fast as samba. From what I can tell, the re-read performance difference is due to FUSE and userspace mounting not being able to use kernel level caching. The setup, use, and performance of SSHFS is easier than samba. While it will never replace NFS, it's a better choice than samba.”
DD Write dd if=/dev/zero of=onegig bs=1024 count=1000000 (1.0 GB) copied, 160.725 seconds, 6.4 MB/s DD Read dd if=onegig of=/dev/null bs=1024 (1.0 GB) copied, 94.223 seconds, 10.9 MB/s
IOZone ./iozone -Rac -i 0 -i 1 -b hutz2.xls Writes average is: 6671.531746 KB/s Re-writes average is: 6594.031746 KB/s Reads average is: 10334.2619 KB/s Re-reads average is: 10330.34921 KB/s
Unless you are doing a lot of re-reads, SSHFS is at least twice as fast as samba. From what I can tell, the re-read performance difference is due to FUSE and userspace mounting not being able to use kernel level caching. The setup, use, and performance of SSHFS is easier than samba. While it will never replace NFS, it's a better choice than samba.
Testing was performed on a fully patched Ubuntu Edgy system using kernel version 2.6.17.

Ethical Use of Computing Resources
Ethical Use of Computing Resources
Table of Contents
Ethical Use of Computing Resources
INTRODUCTION
COMPUTER ACCOUNTS
ETHICAL BEHAVIOR
DISCIPLINARY PROCEDURES
GOOD CITIZENSHIP
ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
Ethical Use of Computing Resources
INTRODUCTION
As a student you  are  probably  aware  of  certain  ethical
responsibilities  you  have, such as honesty in doing class-
work.  Another area in  which  you  have  important  ethical
responsibilities  is  in  your  use  of computing resources.
This document describes some of these  responsibilities  and
explains  ICS  Department policy on student use of computing
resources.  Some of these policies might be  different  from
what  you  would  expect, so please read over and understand
this document.
The ICS department provides for you,  the  student,  a  wide
range   of  computing  resources from X-Terminals to PC's to
large multiuser UNIX systems.  These machines are  expensive
to   buy  and  expensive  to maintain, but it is our goal to
provide you with the very best computing environment that we
can.    Many users depend on these computers for doing class
assignments, research, and for communications.  We   are   a
community  of computer users,  and like any community we can
all make the best use of  our  resources  if  we   establish
some  guidelines for how we can use them responsibly.
Some computing facilities, such as those which hold  classified data, may establish expensive and complex security systems.  In our department we do not do  this;  we  have  some
security mechanisms which greatly decrease the risk that one
user will accidentally interfere with another, but  it  does
not  require great ingenuity to get around these mechanisms.
As a result, we  need  to  trust  the  people  who  use  our
machines.
The fundamental principle behind our policies is this: While
using the computers, you should never do anything that harms
another user or prevents him or her from getting work done.
If you have any questions  about  these  rules,  or  if  you
suspect  that an account (your's or someone else's) has been
broken into, please contact the ICS Computing Support Group.
To  do  this,  send  mail  to  the address support, or go to
Support's office, CS346, and explain the problem.
COMPUTER ACCOUNTS
The ICS Department has a wide range of computers  available,
located  in  several  different labs. Some of these labs are
open only to people enrolled in certain  classes,  some  are
available  for  general drop-in use. Each ICS major is given
both  an  NT  account,  and  a  UNIX  account  on  the   Sun
workstations.   Different  courses  will  require  different
platforms to be used. Non-majors will be given accounts only
if required for a course in which they are enrolled.
All ICS computers are to  be  used  only  by  ICS  students,
faculty, and staff.  People outside ICS who wish to use computers should go see the people in the  Office  of  Academic
Computing  (Engineering  Gateway,  room 2130).  They provide
computing for non-ICS people.
Any computer account created for you remains the property of
the  Regents  of  the  University  of  California.   You are
responsible for this account, and  you  may  not  allow  any
other person to use it.  The primary purpose of your account
is to allow you to carry out your computing assignments  and
other  instructional  activities.   You may also make modest
use of these resources for other purposes, such  as  sending
electronic mail to friends on campus, reading the electronic
bulletin boards, and playing games, provided that this usage
does  not  significantly interfere with instructional use of
the machines.
An example of how one might “significantly interfere”  would
be be  to  tie  up a computer for game-playing when no other
computers are free and someone else is waiting to   use  the
computer  to  do an assignment.  If you have a game or other
program you would like to make  available  to  other  users,
please  give  it  to  the  system  administrator  for public
installation (You can contact the  system  administrator  by
sending  mail to support).  You may not use the machines for
commercial purposes, such as preparing bills for  your  company or advertising products, or for work related to non-UCI
organizations, such as an off-campus political or  religious
group.  More details about this are given below.  If you are
in doubt about whether some use of the machines is  allowed,
ask the Support Group.
ETHICAL BEHAVIOR
Here is a list of  some  examples  of  activities  that  the
department  does not allow.  If a student makes such unethical use of ICS computers, he/she  will  be  subject  to  the
penalties described in the Disciplinary Procedures section.
You may not introduce viruses,  worms,  Trojan  horses,     password  cracking  or  login  spoofing programs on any     University computer or network.  In  fact,  because  of     the  serious  damage  such  programs can cause, the ICS      Department faculty have adopted a policy which  forbids      students  even to have these types of programs in their      accounts or to place them onto any Department computer;      you  may  not  store  such  a program on a departmental      computer even if you only wish to study it.
You may not try to use equipment or accounts  that  you      are not allowed to use.
You may not interfere with others' ability to make  use      of  the resources.  For example, it might be reasonable      to lock a terminal if you need to leave  the  room  for      two  or three minutes, but it is not reasonable to lock      it while you leave to buy lunch.  Another example would      be  doing  something  that ties up all or a significant      fraction of the machine, thus  preventing  others  from      receiving their fair share.
You may not destroy other people's work.
You may not “spy” on  people,  that  is,  you  may  not      attempt to gain information from their accounts or from      their diskettes when there is good  reason  to  believe      that  they  do not wish you to obtain that information.      This includes both attempting to violate the protection      facilities provided by the system and also taking deliberate advantage of someone else's failure  to  protect      sensitive  information  on  their  account.  This works      both ways.  For example, it would be  unethical  for  a faculty  member  or  Support  Group  member  to  browse      through your personal messages just out  of  curiosity, even  if they have a security level that allows them to      do so; we agree to respect your privacy.  We  do,  however,  retain  the  right  to  inspect material on your      account  when  this  is  necessary  to  investigate   a      suspected  violation  of  university  rules,  such as a      cheating incident or a violation of the rules  in  this      document.
You may not send mail that appears to come from someone      else.
You may not advertise any commercial  products  or  use      your account to earn money.  Non-commercial things like      posting your used car advertisement on  ics.market  are      permitted,  though.   If  for  some  reason you need an      account that can be used for commercial  purposes,  see      the Office of Academic Computing.
You may not display offensive material in any  publicly      accessible  area.         There are materials available      on the Internet and elsewhere that some members of  the      ICS  community  will  find  offensive.  (One example is      sexually explicit graphics; another is political  argument  on such issues as abortion.)  ICS and the University are committed to maintaining  the  free  and  open      exchange  of  ideas  as well as a non-offensive working    environment.  Thus, ICS does not restrict  the  availability  of potentially offensive material, but ICS does      regard  as  unethical  conduct  the  display  of   such      material  in any publicly accessible area, including on      terminal screens in public terminal rooms and  in  computer labs.
You may not use  the  computers'  printers  as  copying machines.  For  example, you may not print out one hun dred copies of a report; instead, print out one copy of the  report  and  use  a  copying machine to obtain the other 99.
DISCIPLINARY PROCEDURES
What happens if you violate any of these rules?  It  depends
on  the seriousness of the offense, but could be one or more
of the following.   Disciplinary  procedures  and  sanctions
will  be consistent with those outlined in the UCI Implementation of “Interrim  Policies  and  Procedures  Applying  to
Campus Activities, Organizations, and Students, Part A.”
You may have to meet with the chair  of  the  Computing Resources Committee (CRC), the chair of the ICS Depart ment, or the manager of the ICS Support Group  to  dis cuss abuse of computing resources.
Your account may be locked.  (Again,  we  recognize  an obligation  to respect your rights as well.  No student account will be locked without discussion and  approval of the chair of ICS, or the chair of the CRC, except in the case of security violations.  It would not be ethical for us to lock your account capriciously; for example, we agree not to lock it simply because you send  a message  to  a bboard expressing disagreement with some Department policy or action.)
For minor infractions, some form of  departmental  services  (e.g., cleaning the screens on terminals) may be requested in exchange for unlocking the account.
For offenses  involving  abusing  computing  resources, cheating  on  course related work, or preventing others from working on assignments, your grade may be  lowered in the class or you may receive a failing grade.
For severe offenses, or repeating minor  offenses,  you may  lose  access to all ICS computing facilities for a period of time.  Access to computing can be denied  for a  limited  time  (e.g., one week, the remainder of the quarter, an entire quarter) or permanently.
You may be suspended or dismissed from the University.
In serious cases your name and  a  description  of  the violation  may  be  reported to the police.  California Penal Code Section 502 makes certain computer abuses  a crime, and penalties can range up to a $10,000 fine and up to three years in prison.
GOOD CITIZENSHIP
Your cooperation in the following areas will  help  us  make
efficient  use  of  the  computing  resources and will avoid
unnecessary impositions on the time of faculty,  staff,  and
other  students.   These are not the sort of things which we
can expect to enforce rigidly; rather, we  are  asking  your
cooperation  for  the benefit of the whole departmental community.  Violations of these guidelines would not ordinarily
result  in  any  of the penalties listed above beyond number
one, unless they were especially flagrant or  persist  after
faculty or staff have asked you to stop.
Please be careful not to use the computer to annoy people, for example by sending them messages which they do not wish to receive.  (The mail system makes it  rather easy to send a message to a very large group of people; please be responsible in your use of  this  capability. In  particular,  when  you reply to a message sent to a large group, avoid cc'ing  your  reply  to  the  entire group unless it is a matter of interest to them.)
Please do not waste anything (i.e., paper, disk  space, CPU  time,  people  time,  etc.).   Please put your old printouts in the recycling bins.
ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
Some of these polices are adapted from  those  used  by  the
UCLA CS Department.  They adapted some of their polices from
Columbia University and the California Institute of Technology.

Welcome to Bren ICS!
Hello and welcome to the Donald Bren School of Information and Computer Sciences (Bren ICS).
You are receiving this message because you successfully created an ICS account with Bren ICS.
Bren ICS has a variety of resources available to assist you with your studies and research, and this message will provide a brief tour of these resources.
Getting Started
Quick Tip: Use our 24/7 chatbot in the bottom right corner of this page for AI guided answers from our wiki!
Using ICS Computing Resources
Computer Labs
Bren ICS has four computer labs available for use by Bren ICS students. The computers in these labs will generally have the software required for your Bren ICS courses.
The three computer labs located on the first floor of the CS building (CS 183, CS 189, and CS 192) are reserved for scheduled classes. In the computer lab located on the third floor of the CS building (CS 364), we have a Laptop Instructional Lab (CS 364a) and a dedicated “open lab” with no scheduled classes (CS 364b). Bren ICS students can use this lab on a first-come, first-served basis.
For more information about Bren ICS computer labs, see the following links:
Lab Schedule
Software
Hardware
Printing
There is a black-and-white laser printer available for Bren ICS students in the CS 364b open lab. You must have credit available on your student ID card in order to print.
For more information on using this printer, see:
CS 364b printer information
Information about print credits
WiFi
The student wireless network covering Bren ICS is managed by the UCI Office of Information Technology (OIT). Your wireless device will need to be registered with OIT before it can connect to the wireless network.
To register your wireless device, go to
UCInet Mobile Access
.
Connecting from Off-Campus
🏠➡️🏫
Remote Access Support
-  Access your ICS resources securely from home or any remote location.
To access Bren ICS computing resources from off-campus, you will need to connect to a virtual private network (VPN) managed by the UCI Office of Information Technology (OIT). Please note that the campus wifi and on-campus residential housing are considered to be off-campus networks and will also require a VPN connection to access some Bren ICS resources.
For more information on the OIT VPN and the software needed to connect to the VPN, visit
UCI Virtual Private Network
.
Mac AnyConnect installation issues: If your AnyConnect stops working, try deleting your current installation.  Download a new copy of the software from OIT.  If you have problems re-installing, in a terminal window run the command:
sudo pkgutil –-forget com.cisco.pkg.anyconnect.vpn
Storage
Bren ICS provides professionally-managed data storage for all Bren ICS students. This storage can be accessed from all computers in the Bren ICS computer labs, from all servers in the Bren ICS Linux cluster, and from your own personal computers.
For more information about Bren ICS storage and how to access it, see the following links:
Accessing Storage
Restoring files and folders
Space limits
Linux / Openlab / SLURM
Bren ICS maintains a cluster of Linux servers known as Openlab which are available to all ICS users.  These servers can be accessed via SSH.  We also offer SLURM, a job scheduling system to allow fair access to computing clusters for instruction and research.
For more information, see the following links:
Openlab Linux Cluster @ ICS
SLURM information
Personal Web Page
Bren ICS provides basic web hosting functionality so that ICS students can host web pages needed for academic projects, as well as web pages for personal non-commercial use.
For setup information, see
How to set up your web page
.

ICS Modules.

ICS Password Overview
ICS Single Sign-on
ICS uses
single sign-on
for all ICS services including Windows login, Linux shell accounts, and ICS email services. This means you only need to remember one set of credentials to access most ICS resources.
Your
ICS account credentials
are distinct and different from your UCInetID credentials. Some ICS applications require UCInetID credentials and will clearly indicate when to use them for authentication.
Quick Tip: Use our 24/7 chatbot in the bottom right corner of this page for AI guided answers from our wiki!
Reset Your ICS Password
Change Password
- Use this link if you know your old password but would like to change it.
Reset Password via Email
- Use this link if you forgot your password and would like to reset it via Email to your recovery email address.
Reset Password via Secret
- Use this link if you forgot your password and would like to reset it by answering a secret question.
How to reset:
Reset using your
Security Question
or
Recovery Email address
Select your preferred method from the top navigation bar
Your ICS username is the same as your UCInetID
Review and follow the password requirements
For additional help, email helpdesk@ics.uci.edu with your information, problem description, and relevant screenshots
Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is a security method that requires users to provide multiple forms of verification before gaining access.
The university uses an MFA solution called
DUO
.
MFA Requirements:
UCI Resources
: DUO MFA is required for many UCI resources
ICS Resources
: DUO MFA is required for sensitive ICS resources such as outward-facing servers like emp.ics.uci.edu or
Openlab Jupyterhub

ICS server users are
strongly recommended
to set up SSH keys to access ICS computing resources. SSH keys are
required
to connect to some ICS servers from off-campus when not using VPN.
Learn more
:
SSH Keys
Change or Reset Your ICS Password
You may reset your ICS password at
ICS Password Page
(password.ics.uci.edu) if you:
Know your current password,
OR
Have set up and can answer your security question,
OR
Have access to your alternate email address, which will be used to send a password reset message
You may send email to helpdesk@ics.uci.edu for help recovering your alternate email address.
When visiting the password site to reset your password, please note the links along the top bar:
Click on either
Question
or
Email
to reset a forgotten password.
To reset by email
You can reset your password by going to:
https://password.ics.uci.edu
If you are off campus, including campus housing, you will need to connect to
the
campus VPN
first.
-Enter your username: (should be the same as your UCInetID)
-Enter your recovery address: (this was set when you created your account)
Our system will send your recovery address an email with a password reset link.
Contact helpdesk@ics.uci.edu if you need help determining your recovery address.
Types of Passwords Reset
The ICS Password Toolbox will change the following passwords:
ICS LDAP
ICS Google Workspace (for non-undergraduate accounts)

Password Management Tools
Password management tools aid in securely storing and conveniently recalling passwords. See a list of password/secret managers here:
Password/Secret Management
There are additional password managers available, but these are the ones that Computing Support has used in the past and can recommend:
KeePass Password Safe
Locally-managed encrypted password file
The KeePass program is distributed under the GNU GPLv2
1Password
Cloud-based
Convenient browser plugins available
Convenient family options at no charge
Choosing a Password
Create a password using a combination of lowercase, uppercase, symbol, and number characters
Use a longer password (at least 8 characters in length)
Use a “passphrase” so that your password is easy to remember, for example YoUc@nnOts3e! for “You cannot see!”
How Often to Change Passwords
ICS Computing Support will require that you change your password under the following circumstances:
A significant change to the way that passwords are stored is implemented
There is reasonable suspicion that your account has been compromised
It is also a good idea to change your password after certain foreign travel, any use of unsecured networks, and any time that your computer becomes infected with viruses/malware.
Troubleshooting
Password was refused by the LDAP directory
If you receive the message “Password was refused by the LDAP directory”, this typically means that the server running the Password Reset page doesn't have permission to make changes to the LDAP database.  Send email to helpdesk@ics.uci.edu.

🏫 Duo in ICS
ICS Computing Support uses Duo multi-factor authentication for sensitive systems and management tools.
Need Duo for your systems?
Email
helpdesk@ics.uci.edu
if you are interested in using Duo on ICS systems or research equipment.
📱 Connecting Without the Phone App
Duo MFA settings on certain bastion or gateway hosts have been configured to accept the use of a
DUO_PASSCODE
to provide service to users with hardware tokens and poor phone connectivity.
Important
: If you are required to use a passcode when connecting to a bastion host, please set up
SSH keys
to login to other ICS hosts without using Duo MFA.
🔑 Duo Passcode Configuration
Users may force the auto-push behavior to their phone or tablet app by setting and exporting the following variable:
export DUO_PASSCODE=push
Note
: There is no longer a way to skip MFA entirely when using
SSH keys
on bastion hosts.
🚪 Login Process
Users enrolled in Duo will be presented with the following menu when logging in:
Duo two-factor login for ____

Enter a passcode or select one of the following options:

 1. Duo Push to XXX-XXX-XXXX
 2. Duo Push to Android

Passcode or option (1-2):
Authentication Options:
Duo app users
: Choose option 1 to receive a push notification to your phone
Hardware token/passcode users
: Enter your 6-digit passcode directly
SSH Keys Note
: All users logging into
emp.ics.uci.edu
will receive this prompt regardless of whether they are using
SSH keys
. Currently, this change only applies to
emp.ics.uci.edu
, and multi-factor authentication prompts will not appear on other hosts for users using SSH keys.
⚙️ SendEnv DUO_PASSCODE Configuration
macOS or Linux SSH Client
In your environment, set the
DUO_PASSCODE
variable (Linux /bin/bash shown):
export DUO_PASSCODE=push
Add the following to
~/.ssh/config
or
/etc/ssh/ssh_config
:
SendEnv DUO_PASSCODE
Note
: This configuration has already been added to all ICS CentOS7 hosts.
Direct SSH Command Line
Use the
-o SendEnv=DUO_PASSCODE
option:
ssh -o SendEnv=DUO_PASSCODE username@emp.ics.uci.edu
PuTTY Configuration
Navigate to
Connection → Data
:
Enter
DUO_PASSCODE
as the
variable
Enter
push
as the
value

Duo Desktop Troubleshooting
Chrome Permissions:
Chrome requires permission to access apps on your device. When prompted, select Allow.
If Duo Desktop is installed but not detected:
If you receive a prompt to install Duo Desktop even though it is already on your machine, follow these steps:
Verify with Firefox: Check if the same message appears in Firefox. If it does, you must uninstall and reinstall Duo Desktop.
  Reset Chrome Permissions: After reinstalling, you may need to reset the connection in Chrome:
      Go to Chrome Settings > Privacy and Security > Site Settings.
      Locate and select Duo Security.
      Click Delete Data and Reset Permissions.
Required vs. Optional Duo Authentication
Duo host certificates come in two types:
Required Two-Factor
: Users
MUST
use MFA to login
Opportunistic Two-Factor
: Users
MAY
use MFA if enrolled
Configuration Issue
: If a server set to “Allow Access” doesn't behave correctly, you may see this error:
Aborted Duo login for '<user>' from host: Access Denied. 
The username you have entered cannot authenticate with Duo Security. 
Please contact your system administrator.
This requires Computing Support to contact OIT for configuration changes.
Account Lockout
Symptoms
: Error in
/var/log/secure
on the target server:
Mar 26 08:59:05 hostname sshd[31254]: Aborted Duo login for 'username' 
from client: Your account has been locked out due to excessive 
authentication failures. Please contact your administrator.
Solution
: Contact OIT Helpdesk to unlock your account:
📧 Email:
oit@uci.edu
📞 Phone:
949-824-2222
🎫 Submit a ticket (include your mobile number)
Test
: Use the
token test page
to check if you're locked out.
No Default Factor Error
Error
: “Aborted Duo login: No default factor found for automatic login”
Solution
: Complete the Duo signup process at the
Duo Support Desk
.
No Duo Prompt
Error
:  “Check your device for duo” then “Invalid username, password, or DUO timed out.”
Solution
:  If you registered multiple devices, like an iPad and your cell phone, check to see which one is getting the Duo prompt.
No Duo Prompt for hub.ics.uci.edu
Error
:  You are not being Duo prompted when trying to log in to hub.ics.uci.edu.
Solution
:  Check this
page
(hint: use password,passcode for the Password field).
Android Phone Prompting For Duo
To prevent the Duo Desktop installation prompt, ensure the browser is not set to Desktop mode.
Disable desktop mode globally
1. Open Chrome on your Android device.
2. Tap the three-dots menu in the top-right corner and select Settings.
3. Under the “Advanced” section, tap Site settings.
4. Tap Desktop site.
5. Slide the switch to the off position (it should turn gray).
OR Disable desktop mode for a specific site
7. Open Chrome and navigate to the website.
8. Tap the three-dots menu and select Settings.
9. Go to Site settings.
10. Tap Desktop site.
11. Tap + ADD SITE EXCEPTION.
12. Enter the website's
URL
and choose the desired setting to prevent it from using desktop mode by default.
Need Help?
Contact ICS Computing Support at
helpdesk@ics.uci.edu
for assistance with Duo configuration or troubleshooting.

ZotDefend Resources @ ICS
ZotDefend is an initiative by UC Irvine to enhance cybersecurity across campus by implementing new security standards, ensuring compliance through mandatory training, and deploying advanced security measures to comply with UCOP-mandated targets.
OIT Resources
ZotDefend Project
- OIT overview and links
UC Cyber Security Mandate
- The UC President shared a letter detailing cybersecurity requirements that every UC location is expected to achieve by May 2025, including 100% compliance
FAQ
- ZotDefend Security Package: Content, Functionality, and Privacy
FAQ
Package Enforcement
- Current list of services that require a computing device with the ZotDefend Security Package installed to access
ICS Interpretation and Policy
All university-owned computers must have the minimal ZotDefend components installed.
ICS Computing Support is tracking ZotDefend uptake and reporting to campus security on a quarterly basis.
ZotDefend Self-Enroll Security Packages are available to faculty and staff who manage their own devices.
All university-owned equipment that is installed or configured by the ICS Computing Support team will be enrolled in ZotDefend with the minimal subset of ZotDefend packages installed.
Who Must Use ZotDefend
Any staff or faculty member using a computer running a supported operating system or an operating system capable of running the ZotDefend package. Feel free to contact ICS Computing Support at
helpdesk@ics.uci.edu
with questions.
Services Requiring ZotDefend
OIT maintains a
list of services
that require use of a computing device with the
ZotDefend Security Package
installed to access. At this time, the list includes:
Kuali Financial System (KFS)
Security Access Management System (KSAMS)
DocuSign
Atlassian (i.e., Confluence, Jira, etc.)
OneTrust
UCI Reserved VPN group
OIT Departmental VPN groups
FacNet
Canvas
Time Reporting System (TRS)
Supported Operating Systems
This is not an exhaustive list, but some examples of supported operating systems include the following. Please contact
helpdesk@ics.uci.edu
if you have a question about a specific operating system.
Windows 11
macOS
- Follow this link for OIT's information
Ubuntu 24.04 LTS and newer LTS versions (Ubuntu 22.04 is
EOL
in the first quarter of 2027 and will no longer be supported)
Unsupported Operating Systems
This is a non-exhaustive list of operating systems that do not support the ZotDefend package. In some cases, this is because one or more components haven't been released for the operating system; in other cases, it is because the
OS
is End of Life and no longer receiving security patches.
ChromeOS
iOS
Android
Windows 10 and earlier
macOS 12 and earlier
Ubuntu 22.04 LTS and earlier
Exempted Classifications
The following classifications are not required to use ZotDefend unless they are using a university-provided device:
Students (Graduate and Undergraduate)
Postdoctoral Scholars (PX)
Academic Researchers (RA)
Unit 18 Titles
Public Education Specialist Titles
Admissions Evaluators
Emeriti
UCI Health Community Network
Linux Research Hosts
University-owned Linux hosts are required to run the minimal ZotDefend components.
Please follow the ICS installation instructions for Nessus below. Failure to do so will place your host into a non-ICS managed group.
FireEye
- Endpoint protection. This tool can detect connections from bad actors and identify suspicious local activity.
Nessus Agent
- Vulnerability scanning. This tool detects out-of-date packages and other local vulnerabilities. Please follow the ICS directions to ensure that the host is grouped with other ACA_ICS hosts.
Desktop Linux
Faculty who use a Linux desktop computer to access campus resources will require additional ZotDefend packages such as Duo Desktop and may contact
helpdesk@ics.uci.edu
for advice. Duo Desktop supports current operating systems such as Ubuntu 24.04 LTS.

Builtin Firewalls

OS

firewalld

Fail2Ban
Fail2ban scans log files (e.g. /var/log/apache/error_log) and bans IPs that show the malicious signs.  Once a malicious client has been identified fail2ban uses the local firewall to block further connections from the client for a brief (configurable) period of time.
Fail2ban home
ICS Fail2ban information
Rootkithunter
Use the Rootkit Huner project package to notify you regarding suspicious system changes.
Rootkit Hunter Project
rkhunter @ ICS
Campus VPN
Use the campus VPN, or run your own, to limit the number of ports that are open to the internet at large and ensure the security of your data event when using an unsecured WIFI network.
Use the campus VPN to connect to your system from off campus, campus wifi network, or student housing.
Configure a self managed
OpenVPN
for improved ease of use.
Never Use Plaintext Protocols
Never use a plain-text protocol such as telnet, ftp, or rsh.
Always connect to a VPN first, make sure you connecsion traverse the VPN.
Other Links
Other ideas for encrypting traffic:
ssh tunneling:
(done correctly the client would simply `mysql -h localhost`)
http://www.revsys.com/writings/quicktips/ssh-tunnel.html
openssl tunnel
https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials/how-to-set-up-an-ssl-tunnel-using-stunnel-on-ubuntu
Securing MySQL
All off campus MySQL clients would first connect to the
campus VPN
before making a connection to a MySQL server.  Opening MySQL services at the campus border is strongly discouraged because it exposes a attractive attack target and because data, including user credentials, are transmitted plaintext.
Secure MySQL
If using the campus VPN is not an option for some reason, consider using MySQL with openSSL.
https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.7/en/secure-connections.html
A quick look at my own machine says that MySQL SSL is avaiable in the default mysql-server package for Ubuntu 16.04:
https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials/how-to-configure-ssl-tls-for-mysql-on-ubuntu-16-04
Secure MySQL Access Control List
Since this system is exposed to the internet, please use very specific
ACL
's (e.g. 'non-root'@'128.195.14.151').
Secure with IPTables
Also prevent attacks by blocking all unknown connections to this port via iptables.  Use rules similar to the these:
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -s <ip-address> --dport 3306 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 3306 -j DROP

Relinking
In order for the helpdesk team to read the scan reports from the Nessus client scans, the host must be part of the ACA_ICS groups. The following commands will relink your client to the Nessus scanner and ensure that it is assigned to the ACA_ICS groups.
Start by unlinking:
/opt/nessus_agent/sbin/nessuscli agent unlink
Then link the agent again with the groups option:
/opt/nessus_agent/sbin/nessuscli agent link \
  --host=nessus.oit.uci.edu --port=443 \
  --key=84bd6d8fb397eab26eeac5da94090cd74601095515492d14e7658defaa702084 \
  --groups=ACA_ICS
Start the Service
Issue the following command as root to ensure the service is enabled and started:
systemctl
enable
nessusagent
systemctl start nessusagent

systemctl status nessusagent

UISL Low Risk Supplier Review Program

, you can now submit your request directly to
helpdesk@ics.uci.edu
How to Submit
Download and complete the
Supplier Security Review Questionnaire (SSRQ)
Email PDF to helpdesk@ics.uci.edu to request a supplier security review from ICS Helpdesk.
Monitor email updates from ICS Helpdesk as triage the request as low risk or high risk. If any answers are “unsure”, ICS Helpdesk will follow up with you to clarify.
If determined as low risk, we will conduct the review.  Once it passes review, ICS Helpdesk will respond to you and cc low-risksupplierrev@exchange.uci.edu.  This is sufficient for proof of review for purchasing purposes.
If determined as high risk and further review is required, ICS Helpdesk will forward it to securityreviews@uci.edu on your behalf.

Trellix FireEye Endpoint Protection by OIT
Notes and Observations by pnimsomb
The anti-virus portion of FireEye seems to only be activated/enabled/installed for clients that have an ICS IP address range. By default (for the rest of the campus), FireEye is only set for threat detection, not with AV. Ryan Bakonis (before leaving UCI) mentioned there needs to be a common identifier for OIT Security to create a policy, so we settled on IP address range when Sophos licensing expired.
As of July 2022, the simplest way to get FireEye with the AV part installed/activated is to physically plug in the desktop/laptop being set up to an ICS managed ethernet connection. If the device has no ethernet port, try one of the USB-to-ethernet adapters. Alternatively, the WiFi within the Support suite area, ICS Zone, will also give an IP address within the ICS range. Setting up a computer using the main campus WiFi (UCInet Mobile Access or eduroam) will only enable the default threat detection portion. If a computer has already been setup with only the threat detection portion, then email security@uci.edu to request the AV portion be manually enabled/installed. OIT Security will need some form of identifier, so something like an IP address or hostname.

or

Install the ICS custom repo on your Ubuntu host by running the following as root:
cat << EOF >> /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ics-custom.list
deb [trusted=yes arch=amd64] https://repo.ics.uci.edu/ubuntu/ jammy main
EOF
apt update
CentSO7
Running the following block of code will add the ICS-custom repository, and set it disabled, to a CentOS7 host as root.
cat << EOF >> /etc/yum.repos.d/ICS-custom.repo``[ICS-custom]  
name=CentOS-7 - ICS Custom Applications
baseurl=ftp://ftp.ics.uci.edu/pub/centos0/ics-custom-repo  
enabled=0
gpgcheck=0  
priority=10
EOF
Other Operating Systems
Please follow the directions in the
Trellix Endpoint Security Installation documentation
.
Install the Package
Once the ICS-custom repo has been installed, the following
OS
specific commands may be issued to install the package.
Ubuntu 20.04+
apt update 
apt install xagt
CentOS7
yum clean all 
yum --enablerepo=ICS-custom install xagt
Configure
In order to configure you client, please download the attached agent_config.json and then run the following command as root:
/opt/fireeye/bin/xagt -i agent_config.json
Start the Service
Ensure the service has been started as root:
systemctl enable xagt systemctl start xagt
Confirm
CentOS7 and Ubuntu 20.04 LTS and newer
Execute systemctl commands as root:
systemctl status xagt

Notes (from Ryan Bakonis, rbakonis@uci.edu)
The HX client software needs to talk to the managing cloud based server.  
  All updates, including the software, policies and protection rules as well as engines are updated via this mechanism.  
  This also allows us to track licensing as well. 
  Verify that your endpoint (server or workstation) has connectivity outbound to the internet to the required URL via ports 80/443. 
  URL: hexshj633-hx-agent-1.hex01.helix.apps.fireeye.com
  Conduct a software inventory and list any application to be whitelisted to OIT Security? 
  Common software to whitelist would be any existing antivirus software. 
  This will maximize performance and ensure compatibility with other antivirus software.

Issuing a Fixlet via BigFix
section.

Install Steps
To manually install agent software on an individual Windows or macOS endpoint:

Other Notes
Faculty laptops are eligible if they are university-owned. Personal devices are excluded.
The HX management server is cloud-hosted, so there aren’t any on-campus dependencies. The only requirement is that the endpoint can establish an outbound connection to the HX server over the Internet.
The installer can be passed to users for self-service installation.
As of September 2021, UCOP covers 10,000 licenses of FireEye and according to Ryan, UCI has used a little less than half of the allocation so far.
Contact Ryan Bakonis at rbakonis@uci.edu for inquiries relating to the console. Sub-delegation of console access is currently being worked on.
The FireEye installation on BigFix is currently not set to include AV protection.  If installing FireEye from BigFix and AV protection is desired, email Ryan with the computer name to have enabled.
Make sure to remove all other AV protection (such as Sophos) before enabling the AV portion of FireEye
ICS Host Sets
From Benny Angeles of OIT Security (November 7, 2024):
Currently, there are several groups of hosts (also known as "host sets") in our Trellix console pertaining to ICS. They are:

1. ICS Domain (dynamic host set, currently 362 hosts)
2. ICS Host Prefixes (dynamic host set, currently 47 hosts)
3. ICS Machines (static host set, currently 12 hosts)

Dynamic host sets have the ability to automatically add hosts to themselves based on certain attributes of the host. 
For example, the "ICS Domain" dynamic host set grabs every host under the UCI-ICS domain that has the Trellix agent installed. 
So if you were to install the agent on a brand new machine under the UCI-ICS domain, once the agent communicates with the Trellix console, that host would automatically join the "ICS Domain" dynamic host set and obtain the custom policy applied to it.

Static host sets are sets of hosts that are manually created and that do not change, but custom policies can be applied to these as well.

The current configuration for the "ICS Domain" dynamic host set is:
-All hosts under the UCI-ICS domain

The current configuration for the "ICS Host Prefixes" dynamic host set is:
-All hosts that include the following strings in their hostname: Hostname~=/^BAO-|^COMM-|^CS-|^DEANS-|^GSAO-|^SAO-|^STATS-|^TEC-|^ER-|^INFO-/

The "ICS Machines" host set is a static host set so no dynamic configuration exists.

All 3 host sets are receiving the "ICS Workstations" custom policy.

If the configurations for these host sets need to be updated to include other hosts that are not currently included (i.e. add an additional domain, add specific AD OUs, etc) please let me know and I can work with you to update these.

UCI Campus VPN
This page contains information pertaining to the installation and troubleshooting of the UCI Campus VPN from OIT on ICS affiliate work and personal laptops, mobile equipment, and home desktops.
Installation
OIT provides comprehensive instructions on the installation of the campus VPN. The software is available to any ICS community member with a UCINetID. Please see the following
URL
:
OIT UCI Campus VPN
.
Troubleshooting
Follow these steps when troubleshooting general issues with the VPN software:
Verify your credentials
— Ensure that you are using your UCINetID and UCINetID password for authentication. Note that your UCINetID password may be different from your ICS network password.
Check your internet connectivity
— Confirm that you have an active WiFi or cellular data connection to receive Duo two-factor authentication prompts on your mobile device. If you are using a hardware token instead of the Duo Mobile app, you can skip this step.
Restart your computer or mobile device
— A simple reboot can resolve many connectivity issues by clearing temporary network configurations and refreshing system resources.
Restart your duo device
— If you are experiencing issues receiving Duo prompts, restart your phone to refresh the Duo Mobile app and network connections.
Try an alternate VPN server
— If you are currently connecting to
vpn.uci.edu
, try connecting to
vpn2.uci.edu
instead, or vice versa. Different servers may have varying availability or performance.
Change your connection group
— If you are using the UCI-Default group, try selecting a different group from the available options in the VPN client. Different groups may provide better connectivity depending on your network configuration.
Reinstall the VPN software
— If the above steps do not resolve the issue, uninstall the current VPN client completely, then download and install the most recent version from the OIT website:
OIT VPN Download
If you continue to experience problems after following these troubleshooting steps, please contact the ICS Support Center for additional assistance.
Authentication Failed Error
Error Message:
Authentication failed due to problem navigating to the single sign-on URL.
Symptoms:
You do not receive a Duo prompt on your mobile device as expected
Solution:
Reboot your computer
After rebooting, attempt to connect to the VPN again
You should now receive the Duo authentication prompt
VPN Service Unavailable Error
Error Message:
Connect capability is unavailable because the VPN service is unavailable.
Solution:
If on a Mac, see the troubleshooting steps about uninstalling and reinstalling the VPN software.
Uninstall and reinstall the VPN app on your Mac
Uninstall the VPN app using the Cisco's Uninstaller software
If that is not available, drag the Cisco VPN app to your Mac's
Trash
Empty your
Trash
(you cannot undo this action; please check for any deleted files you may need later)
Reboot (do not skip this step)
Sometimes the app is not properly removed from Mac devices, which will prevent you from being able to install it again.  Try:
Open a
Terminal
window (by going to Finder, Applications, Utilities OR searching for it using
Launch Pad
)
Copy paste this command to the
Terminal
window:
sudo pkgutil –-forget com.cisco.pkg.anyconnect.vpn
Press return after entering the command
After successfully uninstalling,
Go to
https://oit.uci.edu/vpn
to download the latest VPN software release
Install the app again (
uncheck everything but
the VPN software)
Immediately after the software is installed, you will need to click
ALLOW
for various security questions.  This is very important for the software to connect properly.
You may also see this message.  Do as it says.
And this one.
Or it may look like this one.
Once you are done “allowing,” ensure that Cisco is turned ON under Login Items.  This is very important.  Otherwise you will get the error “Connect capability is unavailable because the VPN service is unavailable.”
Duo is Not Prompting You
If you are not receiving automatic Duo push notifications, you can manually authenticate using one of the methods below.
Method 1: Using UCI-Classic Group with Duo Passcode
Connect to
vpn.uci.edu
as usual You may need to move or minimize windows to access the VPN connection window 
Click on the Group dropdown menu:
Select UCI-classic from the list of available groups:
Enter your UCINetID credentials in the username and password fields Open the Duo Mobile app on your phone Navigate to your UCI account from your listed accounts Note the 6-digit passcode displayed (these codes expire every 30 seconds, so work quickly) Enter this passcode in the Second Password field (this may also be labeled as “Passcode”) Click Connect to establish the VPN connection
Method 2: Manual Duo Push
Follow steps 1-5 above to connect using the UCI-classic group In the Second Password field, type the word:
push
Press Enter or click Connect This will manually trigger a Duo push notification to your registered device Approve the push notification on your phone to complete the connection
Tip:
If you consistently have issues receiving automatic Duo prompts, consider using the UCI-classic group with manual authentication as your default connection method.
Getting Further Help
If you need help over Bomgar, please contact helpdesk@ics.uci.edu with:
time slots work for you
the error message you are receiving
the operating system (Windows/Mac) you are using
any relevant screenshots

ICS Signage
This page provides an overview of the ICS Signage system.  This system runs screens in the Bren School of ICS, the School of Engineering, and ISEB.
Home
This screen provides an overview of devices that can be managed including name, status, playlist, current ip, and last check-in time.
Actions
Choose an action: Restart or Take Snapshot
Choose 1 or more clients to act on; selected client/s will be greyed out.
Issues
The signage system will let you know if a client has not checked in when expected. We record the last time a client checked in to receive a playlist. That, plus the check-in interval, will let us know if it is late. If the client is late, it shows up on the issues page. Additionally, a client will appear on the issues page if the system was not able to take a snapshot of the screen in the last 20 minutes. The will highlight in red the snapshot time.
Playlists
Defined as a set of files to be played on certain clients between a specified range of dates and times. The files are selected from those stored in the network storage location (/extra/dsign0 ). The files are stored in a hierarchy of subdirectories. The system will scan all directories and present the directories containing image and movie files in a drop-down list. A playlist can be made up of multiple types to be shown. Images, movies, web
URL
, Google Slides, YouTube Playlist. Each of which can have a specified duration, they will be played before moving to the next. Editing rotation in the playlist settings will tell the slide in the playlist how long to run before changing slides.
A playlist can be associated with 1 or more clients. They need to have a valid start date, but the end date can be left blank, ensuring that the playlist never ends. When there are overlapping playlists, the one with the latest start date before the current date/time gets priority.
Clients
Selecting Test Display will allow you to choose from your managed devices and verify what is displayed on the screen. 
Snapshots works similarly, with the difference being that it displays all your devices and the most recent snapshot taken from the check-in time.
Files

Security – It may be necessary to purchase additional hardware to secure the device

ExamTrak
Overview
ExamTrak is a web application designed to capture and track CS Graduate Student Comprehensive Exam Results. The system provides a simple, centralized way to manage exam scores by class and student.
Connection Information:
URL
:
https://support.ics.uci.edu/examtrak
Authentication:
ICS LDAP (automatically determines user role as faculty or student)
Network Access:
Must be on campus or connected to UCI VPN
For VPN setup instructions, visit:
OIT VPN Information
Purpose
The application serves to streamline the comprehensive exam tracking process for the Computer Science department by:
Eliminating the need to maintain manual faculty eligibility lists
Providing automated role-based access control
Centralizing exam result management and review
Integrating with existing UCI systems (GradInfo)
User Roles and Functionality
Faculty
This is the primary application that CS Faculty use to enter the results of
Phase II exams
. Faculty workflow:
Login using ICS LDAP credentials
Select a course and quarter
System automatically imports enrolled students via UNL flat files from the Registrar
Select exam results for each student:
N/A
- Student did not take the exam
Pass
- Student passed the exam
Fail
- Student failed the exam
TBD
- To be determined (result pending)
Adding Unlisted Students:
If a student took the exam but is not on the imported list, faculty can manually add them
Faculty can search for students by entering:
Student ID number
Student name
System searches the ICS LDAP directory to locate and add the student
Faculty can then assign exam results to the added student
Additional Capabilities:
Review and modify results until marked as final
Track results by class, year, and quarter
Students
Students can:
Login to view their comprehensive exam results
See all exam attempts in descending chronological order
Track total number of passed exams
Administrators
Administrators can:
Review all entered exam results
Lock/finalize exam results to prevent further changes
